extra.reasoning: File: /Users/sanjaybasu/clinical-formal-verification/experiments/runs/_judge_prompts_blind/item-963df2256ff5.txt. The property G(opted_out -> not sent) holds. Justification by tracking the only writers of each variable: opted_out is set true only by t1 (optout), and no transition ever resets it to false, so it is monotonic (once true, stays true). t1 simultaneously sets sent := false. sent is set TRUE only by t2, whose guard requires not opted_out; t3 and t4 only ever set sent := false. To violate the property we would need a reachable state with opted_out=true and sent=true. For sent=true, the most recent write to sent must be t2, which requires opted_out=false at that moment. After that, to make opted_out=true we must fire t1, but t1 also clears sent to false. Once opted_out=true it can never return to false, so t2 (the only true-setter of sent) can never fire again; any later send_attempt takes t3 (sent:=false) and any tick takes t4 (sent:=false). Hence in every reachable state where opted_out=true, the last write to sent was a clearing operation (from t1, t3, or t4) and no subsequent setter is enabled, so sent is necessarily false. The invariant is maintained from the initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false) and preserved by every transition, well within the 16-event bound. Therefore the property is never violated.
